title: Google's new Nano Banana 2 Lite image model is its fastest and cheapest yet
gist: Google DeepMind released Nano Banana 2 Lite (Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Image), a fast, cheap image model generating images in ~4 seconds at $0.034 per 1K images, trading some quality (text, consistency) for speed to enable rapid prototyping and mass low-cost generation.

insights.claim: Nano Banana 2 Lite generates a text-to-image output in about 4 seconds in default low-thinking mode versus roughly 20 seconds for standard Nano Banana, a 5x speed improvement.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Sub-5-second generation unlocks real-time iterative UX in design tools; watch which apps adopt it for interactive workflows.
insights.quote: You can go from text to an image in about 4 seconds in the default low-thinking mode. Generating the same images in the standard Nano Banana takes about 20 seconds.

insights.claim: Nano Banana 2 Lite API pricing is $0.25 per 1M input tokens and $1.50 per 1M output tokens, roughly half the rate of Nano Banana 2, averaging $0.034 per 1K images.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Aggressive per-image pricing pressures rivals' low-cost image tiers and lowers the floor for image-generation unit economics.
insights.quote: Google says it averages $0.034 per 1K image. The API rates are $0.25 for 1M input tokens and $1.50 for 1M output tokens. That’s half the rate for Nano Banana 2.
insights.locator: The speed and efficiency mean developers

insights.claim: Nano Banana Pro output tokens cost $12 per 1M, eight times higher than Lite's $1.50, revealing a steep quality-versus-price tiering across Google's image model lineup.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The 8x output-price spread lets developers precisely trade quality for cost per use case; model routing becomes a margin lever.
insights.quote: the output pricing is $12 (eight times higher)
insights.locator: For Nano Banana Pro, the input tokens

insights.claim: Nano Banana 2 Lite scores nearly as high as non-Lite versions on Arena.ai Elo ratings, but struggles with small text, produces infographics with incorrect data, and shows poor character consistency across iterations.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Elo 'vibemarking' overstates usability; the model's text and consistency failures cap its use in production creative and data-viz work.
insights.quote: vibemarking doesn’t always focus on the details that can make AI images look silly upon closer inspection
insights.locator: In addition to the examples, Google also has Elo scores

insights.claim: For single high-quality or photorealistic images with accurate text, the Flash or Pro Nano Banana versions remain preferable, while Lite is positioned for rapid iteration and design inspiration.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Use-case segmentation (draft vs. final) becomes the buying decision; tools may chain cheap Lite drafts into expensive Pro finals.
insights.locator: If you’re just tinkering with an AI model

insights.claim: The cheapness and speed of Nano Banana 2 Lite is expected to increase AI 'slop' online, though all its outputs carry SynthID watermarks that persist through edits to label them as AI.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Cheaper generation accelerates content flooding; SynthID adoption is the key provenance signal to track for detection/verification plays.
insights.quote: The rapid-fire nature of Nano Banana 2 Lite also means there may be a lot more AI slop circulating online.
insights.locator: The rapid-fire nature of Nano Banana 2 Lite

insights.claim: Nano Banana 2 Lite launched simultaneously across Google AI Studio, the Gemini API, and the Gemini app (via Flash-Lite option), while Gemini Omni Flash video generation is expanding to the API and AI Studio.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Broad day-one distribution across Google's developer and consumer surfaces maximizes adoption velocity and lock-in.
insights.locator: You can play with Nano Banana 2 Lite
